  • If you want to drink the “top of the line” Burgundy it will run you $3,000 a bottle in an average vintage and $5,000 in a great vintage.
  • If you want to drink the “top of the line” Barolo it will run you $700 a bottle in an average vintage and $1,200 in a great vintage.

For northern rhone? The “top of the line” syrah in Cornas or Hermitage will run you $250 a bottle in an average vintage and $500 a bottle in a great vintage.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
I think it’s actually a little silly to generalize as much as some of this discussion is doing. I’m not convinced at all that any of the three main N.Rhone appellations can be described in its own general way - it all comes down to the producer. There are wide ranges of soils and expositions in each district, with producers using a wide range of farming and winemaking approaches across them all. You have lower and upper slopes, sometimes plateaus that are not like the slopes at all, orientations of vineyards, organic vs. non, etc., etc. Then you have smaller to larger barrels, old to new oak, other winemaking choices.

Frankly, I gave up on most Cote Rotie some years ago because of the prevalence of new oak in too many wines. While I haven’t been able to get any sense of “Hermitage style” in a long time. For me, Cornas has been, over the past 10-15 years, the place where I found the most interesting and exciting wines, at (until the past few years) more affordable prices. Interestingly, those price escalations are allowing a lot of St Joseph, and even Crozes Hermitage producers to elevate their wine substantially, to the point where I believe those appellations are starting to compete with their big brothers. There are some great soils and old vine vineyards all over the entire region, and just being located in one of the “big three” doesn’t automatically create the best wine. As with other regions, it’s producer, producer, producer.

Cote-Rotie: if no Guigal nor Jamet … Rostaing (Ampodium, La Landonne, Cote blonde), Stephane Ogier (Mon Village, Reserve … if not Belle Helene and Lancement), Gangloff Barbarine, Clusel-Roch classic and Grandes Places, Noel Champet La Viaillere, Henri Gallet Cote blonde … (and more modern producers like Gerin, Monteillet …)

Hermitage: if not JL Chave nor Chapoutier (4 diff. vineyards) nor Jaboulet … Domaine des Remizieres (Emilie), Sorrel (regular and Greal), Colombier … Faurie, Belle …

These wines are all over the map in style: Guigal all overoaked; Ogier also overoaked, though I’ve heard he is dialing that back; Gangloff the poster child for oak; Clusel-Roch a producer really rising in the hierarchy IMO; not familiar with Champet and Gallet. Chapoutier, one word: boring; Jaboulet I have not tried in recent vintages since their long wander through the desert; Remizieres generally too ripe for my tastes; Colombier used to be overoaked, don’t know about recently. etc., etc.

Point being, there is huge variation of wines and styles in each region alone, it’s impossible to make blanket statements about how one of the big three compares to the others. You have to know the producer.

Who are the old small time producers in Hermitage. It seems like big houses rule the day there, while many in Cornas are old school farmers and winemakers, there’s some romance to that which may be missing in Hermitage?

There aren’t many. Belle, Faurie, Ferraton. It’s a very heavily concentrated appellation.

Livingstone-Learmonth’s Wines of the Northern Rhone (2005) lists some others, most of whom never make it to the US. Here is the how Hermitage’s 131 hectares are divided up among producers, with their percentage share, according to L-L. (These are my calculations based on his entries on the producers. This includes white and red, vineyards owned and under contract.)

Chapoutier 31.5 ha - 24%
Jaboulet 26.8 ha - 20%
Chave 13.9 ha - 11%
Delas 10 ha - 8%
Guigal* 3.3 ha - 3%
Sorrel 2.6 ha - 2%

(* This is what Guigal owns. He may well buy other grapes.)

These seven account for 67% of the acreage, and the Tain coop accounts for much of the rest.

Since the 1990s, Chave bought Dom. Gray and Guigal bought Grippat and De Vallouit’s vines.

The Cornas appellation had 104 ha under cultivation in 2004, or roughly 75% of Hermitage’s area. But Cornas was much smaller in the past. Acreage under cultivation doubled from 1971 (53 ha) to 2004, while Hermitage had increased only slightly. I think that’s because a lot of abandoned terraces in Cornas were brought back into use, while pretty much all of Hermitage had been exploited, even before Northern Rhone really caught on in the late 80s and 90s.

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
Hermitage may well be the better piece of dirt over Cornas but it doesn’t help that there is so much mediocre stuff being made on that hill.

These days, I think Chave and Jaboulet own or have contracts on a large portion of the hill, no?

Jamie Goode has a rough list of holdings about halfway down the page here: The Hill of Hermitage: one of the wine world's most famous appellations

The largest landholder on that list is Chapoutigarbage, followed by Jaboulet, whose wines are never ready, and then the Tain Coop (never had the wines and don’t really care to). The list doesn’t have Guigal’s holdings, which should be substantial, just like the oak treatments. Of the grandes marques, Chave is the smallest holder (probably doesn’t include the negociant land), and the only one worth a damn.

posts.cooked: I recently had a Chapoutier 1991 Hermitage and although much of the oak had been absorbed, there was still a little too much for my taste. Big, powerful and straightforward. Some will love; for me drinkable, but not something I would want in my cellar.

Maybe this is the wrong way to look at it but:

  • If you want to drink the “top of the line” Burgundy it will run you $3,000 a bottle in an average vintage and $5,000 in a great vintage.
  • If you want to drink the “top of the line” Barolo it will run you $700 a bottle in an average vintage and $1,200 in a great vintage.
  • If you want to drink the “top of the line” Bordeaux it will run you $700 a bottle in an average vintage and $1,000 a bottle in a great vintage

For northern rhone? The “top of the line” syrah in Cornas or Hermitage will run you $250 a bottle in an average vintage and $500 a bottle in a great vintage.

On that basis, I have zero complaints about the state of rhone pricing. Cornas specifically is only slowly inching towards where Hermitage and Cote Rotie already are and to my tastes Cornas is every bit as good as its counterparts in those other villages.

I have read (and in the past may even have written) analyses like this but they really make no sense to me. I would not assess the value of a VW by comparing its price to that of a Maybach. In a few wine regions, there is a tier at the top where prices are built almost entirely on rarity and label appeal. DRC and Lafleur are as irrelevant to me as that Maybach would be.

One can buy burgundies and bdx of outstanding quality in every vintage for well under $100 and in most for under $75. And very good wines for $50 or less.

For me it is a mistake to define the value of a region (or at least the iconic ones) by the wines at the very top, where they compete with the other LMVH stable of luxury goods rather than the other wines made in their regions.

I have to politely disagree with a few things here.

First I think we owe it to others on this board to dispense with the “there’s still plenty of very good burgundy for $50.” It’s always the folks who have been collecting burgundy for 20+ years that make this claim and everyone newer to burgundy reads it and has no idea what they’re talking about. Yes, if you’re really knowledgeable and have excellent sources, there’s a gem here and there each vintage that is something like $50 and really shines. But the typical person reading this board does not know that wine and can’t source it even if they knew it. You can love or hate burgundy but I would never steer someone to it if they’re looking to stick to a $50/bottle budget.

I get where you’re going with the Maybach analogy but it’s a flawed metaphor. Here’s a different way to put it: I would genuinely enjoy being near the stage of a David Byrne concert more than I would be driving a Ferrari even if the costs were the same. Lucky for me, it costs a lot less to go to the concert than it does to drive the Ferrari. There are folks who wear the David Byrne t-shirt everywhere just like there are folks who like park their Ferrari in front of a restaurant. But then there are folks who genuinely love David Byrne and folks who are genuine Ferrari gearheads. It’s not all conspicuous consumption, some people are genuinely passionate about these things.

posts.name: larry schaffer

Alan,

What I’ve learned on this board, and what gets reinforced all of the time, is that ‘value’ and ‘quality’ are in the eyes of the beholder. No one is right and no one is wrong - we are all just different.

There are some on this board who certainly seem to ‘look down’ upon wines made in the US, just as there are others, such as yourself, who seem to ‘look down’ upon wines made in places like France. I don’t get any of this.

To me, it’s kind of like talking about musical genres. I may not like Country Music, but I will not say it is ‘bad’ or look down upon it - I will simply let others know that I do not prefer or like it. I may listen to it with others who really like it to try to understand it, but what good does it do me to tell them that what they like is ‘bad’?

I’m glad that you’ve found examples of certain varieties here in the US that you’re extremely happy with - regardless of score or praise. I hope that you understand that a wine anointed with a 100 point scores from ANYONE does NOT make it a ‘perfect’ wine - except to that reviewer at that time of tasting. And you just be okay with others saying that they do not like that same wine - and therefore do not find the same value in it.

Let’s try to keep this as ‘logical’ as possible and not head down too many rabbit holes and toss around insults - it’s not ‘becoming’ of those involved in doing so . . .

Cheers.

I’ll admit to snobbery.

I’ll admit to Alfert’s snobbery.

But let’s be honest: California cabs may have walked themselves back from the edge, but they have a justified reputation for having gone high EtOH, high extraction and high oak, especially the “cults” that have driven the market for a long time. I see tons of great cabs (and drink them with some regularity, from Corison to Monte Bello to Maybach) that run the gamut in style but are unimpeachably excellent wines. Corison (and Dunn and Togni and a handful of others) ran against the grain, and a lot of those 90’s and 00’s big wines have fallen apart like Shiraz.

Bordeaux has run the same course, but there are fewer climactic pressures pushing Bordeaux towards 15% and overripeness like there are in California. Rolland is hated and loved in the same breath, and for all the right reasons.

Like many here, I have had dozens of old California cabs from 1959-1996 and love them. As a whole, Similarly aged Bordeaux is better about 75% of the time. Can’t always tell them apart though, for sure.

Maybe this is the wrong way to look at it but:

  • If you want to drink the “top of the line” Burgundy it will run you $3,000 a bottle in an average vintage and $5,000 in a great vintage.
  • If you want to drink the “top of the line” Barolo it will run you $700 a bottle in an average vintage and $1,200 in a great vintage.
  • If you want to drink the “top of the line” Bordeaux it will run you $700 a bottle in an average vintage and $1,000 a bottle in a great vintage

For northern rhone? The “top of the line” syrah in Cornas or Hermitage will run you $250 a bottle in an average vintage and $500 a bottle in a great vintage.

Almost all of that is wrong in my book. For one thing, the single most important factor in getting a “top of the line” experience is drinking the wine at the right time. This isn’t based on how much you spend, but on how patiently you’re able to wait and how good your storage is. That aside, the price numbers you’re using are based on a small number of wines priced that way because of the cachet of the brand, not because they necessarily stand alone at the top in quality. I do agree that makes Chave, the consensus (and deserved) trophy label in Hermitage, a bit of a relative bargain. Cornas currently lacks a trophy label so there’s no $700 version to point to, unless you count old Verset, but that doesn’t mean that $250 (or $75) Cornas is better than $250 (or $75) Burgundy, Barolo, or Bordeaux.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
I notice you didn’t mention st Joseph

You can’t really compare St. Joseph because it’s a weird appellation, a sort of catch-all stretching from Condrieu to Cornas. It’s ~920 hectares, but most of that isn’t remotely comparable to the big three appellations. (It was expanded from less than 100ha in 1971.) So you have Gonon, Chave, Faury and a few others who make really good wine there, but most of St. Joseph is not in the same league. (Click here for a map.)

Same thing with Crozes-Hermitage, at 1,325 hectares. There are a few good producers and sites, but most of the appellation is on a relatively flat plane and isn’t prime.

In Piedmont, the comparable appellation would probably be Langhe, which encompasses lots of different soils and exposures and quality levels.

I agree. A lot of poor wine made in the Northern Rhone.

The Barolo DOC is much larger than Cornas, C-R and Hermitage, (unless you start including Crozes etc) but there is a TON of crap Barolo being made and, while there is a plethora of serious and good mid-range producers in Barolo, the same exists in the Northern Rhone. This thread and others have noted that there are tons of good producers in various styles below the price of Clape and Allemand and Chave.

These are both regions that have undergone serious modernization, improvement and rise in status/desire driven by somewhat controversial use of oak and stylistic changes (Gaja in Barolo and Guigal in Rhône), where now the world market favors the traditionalists more. Lots of producers straddle the line between the two in both regions, most producers are still small scale, and many are dealing with generational changes in terms of winemaking.

I don’t know why folks are so eager to argue that there’s no comparison…

And you can still drink excellent rhone for $50 / bottle or 1/2 to 1/5 the cost of the burgundy examples you cite. For example the 2017 Paris Geynale ran me $40 just a few months ago and the 2016 Voge VV is only a few dollars more. The entire price scale for northern rhone from entry level to most costly is a fair bit better than bordeaux and substantially better than burgundy.

The parallels to piedmont are pretty strong and it makes sense that piedmont has been a little more merciful on the price escalation due to the substantially larger supply. And as Noah pointed out there’s still a lot of mediocre barolo being made that will start to improve now that there’s a price to motivate it.

Everyone has their own easy explanation for what price is due to celebrity/snob value and what is not. Largely it seems to be if it’s a price higher than you’re willing to pay it must be celebrity/snob and if it’s a price you’re willing to pay it’s great QPR.

Having watched a video of multiple Northern rhone growers farm by hand these ridiculously steep slopes that would never be able to be mechanized, I’m a little shocked at how INEXPENSIVE it is compared to the labor.
